original_abstract: The structure and configuration of reaction centers, which fundamentally govern catalytic behavior, often undergo dynamic transformations under operating conditions; however, strategies to exploit these features for enhancing catalytic function remain elusive. Here, we demonstrate a facile light-activation strategy using a TiO2-supported Cu catalyst to regulate the dynamic restructuring of Cu active sites during low-temperature methanol steam reforming. Under dark conditions, the catalyst undergoes thermal deactivation as metallic Cu transforms into inoperative Cu2O. Upon illumination, photoexcited charge carriers from the TiO2 support drive a structural restoration of the Cu sites back to their active metallic state, resulting in a substantial boost to both activity and stability. Under low-intensity solar irradiation, the optimized Cu/TiO2 catalyst achieves a H2 production rate of 1724.1 μmol g⁻¹ min⁻¹, outperforming most conventional photocatalytic and thermocatalytic processes. By leveraging strong light-matter-reactant interactions, we achieve in situ manipulation of the Cu active sites, demonstrating the feasibility of real-time functionalization of catalysts to maintain peak performance.
humanized_abstract: The static structure and dynamic configuration of reaction centers are the core factors that determine the catalytic performance of the catalyst. To this end, it is still a great challenge to utilize the dynamic regulation of catalyst performance. In this work, a simple light-activation method using a TiO2 supported Cu catalyst to regulate the dynamic structural change of Cu active sites during low temperature methanol steam reforming (MSR) was investigated. It was found that the catalyst would be inactivated under light off condition due to the formation of inactive Cu2O from the reduction of Cu metal under dark condition at low temperature. Under UV-LED light activation, the photo-generated charge carriers on the TiO2 surface would instantly reduce the inactive Cu2O to Cu metal, which significantly enhanced the catalytic activity and stability of the catalyst. Under the low intensity solar light, the optimized Cu/TiO2 catalyst showed as high as 1724.1 μmol g⁻¹ min⁻¹ H2 evolution rate, outperforming most of the reported photocatalytic and thermocatalytic activities. Moreover, by means of the strong light-matter-reactant interaction, the Cu active sites can be regulated in situ and in real time, realizing the functionalization of catalysts at their peak performance.
